column1: banana — common fruit with well-known properties
column4: {"category":"Fruit (fresh)","typical_unit":"Each (banana) or 1 medium banana (~7–8 in)","storage_tip":"Store at room temperature to ripen; once ripe, refrigerate to slow further ripening (skins darken but fruit stays sweeter/softer for longer).","nutrition_note":"Bananas provide potassium, vitamin B6, and dietary fiber; they’re more starchy when greener and sweeter when more yellow/spotted."}
column10: {"category":"Fruit","typical_unit":"1 banana","storage_tip":"Store at room temperature until ripe; refrigerate afterward to slow further ripening.","nutrition_note":"Good source of potassium, vitamin B6, and carbohydrates for quick energy."}

column1: gochujang — Korean specialty requiring specific knowledge
column4: {"category":"Korean chili paste (fermented gochujang)","typical_unit":"~1 Tbsp (15 g) or per 1 jar/tub","storage_tip":"Keep tightly sealed; refrigerate after opening. It can last for months to a year—stir before use; if it smells off or shows unusual mold, discard.","nutrition_note":"Typically calories and sodium come from the fermented base and added salt/sugar. Expect moderate carbs (often from sweeteners) and some capsaicin; use small amounts for heat and flavor."}
column8: The output is factually accurate for gochujang: it identifies it as a condiment/paste, gives a typical unit of about 1 Tbsp and mentions jar/tub, provides a reasonable storage tip about keeping it sealed and refrigerating after opening, and includes a nutrition note mentioning fermented base, sodium, carbs, and capsaicin. However, the storage tip does not explicitly distinguish opened vs unopened storage, so it only partially satisfies the rubric.
column10: {"category":"Korean fermented chili paste/condiment","typical_unit":"tub or jar, often 7–16 oz (200–450 g)","storage_tip":"Refrigerate after opening and keep tightly sealed; use a clean spoon to avoid contamination.","nutrition_note":"Flavorful but can be high in sodium and some sugar; a little goes a long way."}
column14: The category, typical unit, and nutrition note are all factually accurate and meet the rubric criteria. However, the storage tip only addresses post-opening storage and fails to distinguish between opened vs. unopened conditions (e.g., unopened gochujang can be stored at room temperature), which the rubric explicitly requires.

column1: quinoa — grain with specific nutritional claims
column4: {"category":"Whole grain / seed (pseudo-grain)","typical_unit":"Dry: 1 cup (about 170–200 g) or 1 lb bag; Cooked: 1 cup (about 185–185 g)","storage_tip":"Store dry quinoa in an airtight container in a cool, dry place (up to ~2 years). After cooking, refrigerate in a covered container up to ~4–5 days; freeze up to ~2–3 months.","nutrition_note":"High in protein and fiber, with important minerals like magnesium and iron. Rinse quinoa before cooking to reduce natural saponins, which can taste bitter."}
column8: While most fields are accurate (category, storage tip with airtight container/cool dry place), the nutrition note fails to highlight a specific distinguishing trait of quinoa such as being a complete protein (containing all essential amino acids) or being gluten-free. Instead, it uses somewhat generic statements about protein and fiber without the specificity the rubric requires.
column10: {"category":"Grain-like seed (pseudo-cereal)","typical_unit":"sold in bags or bulk by the pound/gram","storage_tip":"Store dry quinoa in an airtight container in a cool, dark pantry; for longer storage, keep it in the fridge or freezer.","nutrition_note":"High in protein and fiber for a grain, and it contains all nine essential amino acids; also naturally gluten-free."}

column1: tofu — refrigerated item with specific storage needs
column4: {"category":"Protein (vegetarian/vegan)","typical_unit":"Package (often 14–16 oz / 400–450 g) or block; sold as fresh or shelf-stable; sometimes sold as crumbles or silken packs","storage_tip":"Keep refrigerated after opening (or after thawing). For fresh tofu, store submerged in water in a sealed container and change the water daily; use within 3–5 days. Freeze for longer storage—texture becomes more porous (great for crumbling/adding to soups).","nutrition_note":"Tofu is a good plant-based protein and often contains iron and calcium (especially calcium-set tofu). Nutrition varies by firmness and brand; it’s typically relatively low in carbs and saturated fat, but sodium can be higher in pre-seasoned or marinaded varieties."}
column10: {"category":"Plant-based protein food made from soybeans","typical_unit":"1 block, usually 14–16 oz (about 400–450 g)","storage_tip":"Keep refrigerated in its original packaging until opened; after opening, store submerged in fresh water in a sealed container and change the water daily, or freeze for longer storage.","nutrition_note":"High in protein and a good source of iron and calcium (especially calcium-set tofu); generally low in saturated fat."}
column14: The output is factually accurate for tofu overall, and the storage tip correctly mentions refrigeration and storing opened tofu in water. However, the category is not aligned with the rubric’s expected wording ('produce/refrigerated/protein'), and the nutrition note does not explicitly highlight low calorie; it only mentions protein, iron, calcium, and low saturated fat. Since the rubric requires all four fields to meet those criteria, this is not a full pass.

column1: saffron — expensive spice sold in tiny quantities
column4: {"category":"Spice (saffron threads/stigmas)","typical_unit":"Pinch (threads); commonly sold in small jars or sachets","storage_tip":"Store tightly sealed in a cool, dark, dry place (pantry). Keep away from heat and moisture; for best flavor, use within about 6–12 months after opening.","nutrition_note":"Nutritionally minimal in typical uses (tiny amounts). Provides trace antioxidants (e.g., carotenoids like crocin/crocetin) but not a significant source of calories or macronutrients."}
column10: {"category":"spice","typical_unit":"small jar or threads by the gram","storage_tip":"Store in an airtight container away from light, heat, and moisture.","nutrition_note":"Used in tiny amounts, so it adds very few calories; it mainly contributes flavor and color rather than significant nutrients."}
column14: The category, typical unit, and storage tip are all accurate for saffron. However, the nutrition note fails the rubric requirement — it only mentions low calories and flavor/color contribution, without mentioning saffron's notable antioxidants (crocin, safranal, picrocrocin) or specific bioactive properties.
